data.title: AMD Secured A Solid Backing For A Long-Term Growth Potential
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) continues to enjoy the accelerating demand for its server CPU and Instinct portfolio offerings, delivering strong double-digit financial growth. But the company’s stock price is down almost by 5% since my previous coverage and showcases weak performance since the beginning of the year. Last time, I wrote about two key factors that give AMD a strategic competitive position over Nvidia (NVDA) beyond 2026.

Going forward, the launch of the Rubin generation by Team Green provides a strong validation for AMD’s Helios system. Nvidia brought LPU accelerators and significant adjustments to its new platform, likely in an attempt to cover the solid progress in AMD’s CDNA architecture and ROCm software stack. And with this follow-up, I would like to strengthen my bullish call on AMD, which has the largest contractual agreements for AI accelerators on the market. The company also signalled for a strong supply chain position and production ramp-up, which could unlock at least 15% upside potential for the AMD stock price.

Time to shine

The semiconductor players are going through a harsh market environment since the beginning of the year, as the geopolitical tensions merely add fresh pressure to the already sensitive industry. The main chip stocks have been under increased scrutiny due to the AI bubble fears, spiked by the unprecedented capital investment budgets by large hyperscalers. And while the semiconductor industry is coping with a memory crunch, the Middle East tensions raise further the supply chain concerns.

As a result, Nvidia and AMD fell to the red territory, matching the overall weak performance of the technology sector as a whole. But looking at the financial performance, AMD delivered significant momentum in its data center AI business. The company revealed $10.3 billion in sales for the fourth quarter of 2025, which grew by 34% YoY thanks to the EPYC and Instinct sales.

The data center segment surged by 39% YoY, as the company is executing its MI350 series ramp, supported by the strong adoption of server CPUs from hyperscalers and enterprise customers. It should be noted that AMD is thriving in the CPU market, both in the consumer and the server segments. Team Red is consistently gaining market share thanks to the strong AMD-based instance adoption from cloud providers. At the same time, the Client and Gaming segment went up by 73% YoY, which is not surprising since Ryzen CPUs and RDNA4 GPUs are the most attractive options in terms of cost and performance.

But we cannot bypass the Core Ultra Series 3 launch since Intel (INTC) enters into a revival mode. The Panther Lake generation introduced Intel’s long-awaited 18A chip technology, where the most striking feature became the Xe3 graphics architecture, which delivers a substantial performance gain and supports frame generation. If we look back for a while, Intel has always had a strong position in the laptop segment, while prominently falling behind AMD in the desktop segment with the Raptor Lake and Lunar Lake processors. It seems that Intel landed a strike at the weak point of AMD.

But I am not concerned with AMD’s CPU market position mainly because of the following three points. Partner Lake looks superior to the Stix Point, but the Ryzen AI Max 300 and AI 400 series seem much more competitive. Given the yield of the 18A node and the discrete-level graphics of 12 Xe cores, I don’t think that the consumers would be excited about the Panther Lake pricing. In addition, AMD has leaked its upcoming Zen6 architecture, which delivers Stix point-level performance while running at much lower clocks, enabling full FP16 capabilities. As artificial intelligence is moving toward inference, the general-purpose compute demand is growing substantially in order to handle more workloads, especially for AI agents. Hence, Team Red has very strong growth prospects in its server and client CPU segment with the upcoming Zen6 architecture.

Moving forward to the data center AI business, we saw Nvidia exhibiting its Vera Rubin generation. It seems that Nvidia went all in to secure the future AI inference leadership along with the Groq 3 LPUs. But, from my point of view, Team Green additionally validates the highly competitive nature of AMD’s upcoming rack-scale system. In particular, the MI450X saw a 200W TGP rise from initial figures up to 2500W, and in response, Rubin saw a bump of 500W, reaching up to 2300W. The memory bandwidth of the Rubin platform was increased to 22 TB/s per GPU, up from 13 TB/s. The company is integrating LPUs into the new platform to deliver superior inference performance. At the same time, AMD is preparing a custom rack-scale Helios system, which includes the 6th Gen EPYC Venice CPU, Pensando Vulcano NICs, and the open standard UALink to scale up 72 next-generation MI450 series GPUs for the second half of the year.

Looking at the above chart, it seems that Nvidia is chasing the market momentum, while AMD is not cutting back on research and development investments. As I wrote previously, the main advantage of Nvidia is the timing between architectural generations. Specifically, with the Blackwell architecture, Nvidia remained on the N4 node but doubled the die count per GPU. With the Rubin generation, the company switched to N3 lithography but incorporated the Groq LPX system. On the other hand, AMD is advancing with the raw power and is already a step ahead.

The MI450 series employs the new N2 chip technology, which requires substantial architectural innovation. Moreover, the Helios racks will provide the benefits from the new GAA technology, offering a superior compute and power efficiency. The latter is especially relevant, given that the Helios rack has 50% more HBM4 and the fact that electricity could be a significant bottleneck going forward. And despite higher HBM content and memory crunch, Team Red is more than prepared for the launch in the second half, securing a strong supply chain to ramp up volumes significantly at the end of 2026. The company signed a partnership with Samsung (SSNLF) for next-generation HBM memory, while the Enosimi acquisition will expand the company’s scale-out ability to support and develop a variety of photonics and co-packaged optics solutions across next-gen AI systems.

Valuation and market picture

Looking at the data center segment, AMD booked $16.6 billion in sales, registering a strong 32% annual growth. If the current data center market (including CPU, GPU, and networking) tallies around $180 billion in value, as estimated in my previous article, AMD takes circa 10%. But going forward, the executives of the company seem to have bulletproof expectations for 60% CAGR in data center AI revenue over the next 3 to 5 years. The growth outlook is secured with solid multi-year partnerships with Meta (META) and OpenAI (OPENAI), while in terms of market potential, I believe that it’s more than feasible. Moreover, if we remember the $1 trillion data center TAM by 2030, the 60% CAGR estimate indicates circa 20% market share in AI accelerators and 50% in server CPUs, with data center segment revenue reaching $180 billion.

Following the crunch of the $100 billion deal for Nvidia AI systems, it means that AMD has the largest contractual agreements on the market to deploy AI systems at scale for up to 12 GW of compute power. The analysts are forecasting $47 billion in total sales for the year 2026, including $29 billion of data center revenue. The number seems quite conservative, given the pipeline of 1 GW with each of Meta and OpenAI starting in the second half of the year. We used to think of 1 GW of data center power as $50 billion revenue, but with the increased competition and HBM supply constraints, the figure could be down to $20-30 billion. Hence, the $30 billion data center revenue outlook seems very cautious, given the fact that AMD management sounds very confident regarding the company’s supply chain position and the production ramp.

As a result, I will stick to my $40 billion data center revenue assumption for 2026, since AMD is enjoying the strong momentum for its AI accelerators and server/client CPUs. Moreover, the hyperscaler demand profile is strengthening, with main cloud providers indicating significant investments in AI infrastructure of more than $650 billion in 2026.

Turning to the valuation, AMD is trading at a 26.2x forward P/E ratio, or 15% below the 3Y median. The ratio pulled back substantially from the end of last year, as the concerns regarding the unprecedented data center build-out merely intensified. If we take a closer view, the standard deviation lines on the chart appear like well-known resistance and support levels. We can see that the current valuation of the AMD stock is hanging around the bottom line, and it’s clear to me that the stock price is prominently undervalued.

Investment takeaways

I remain bullish on AMD’s prospects as the stock valuation appears low ahead of the expected growth in 2026. It could be the concerns for the upcoming ramp of the Helios system or the geopolitical and macro uncertainty. But the company secured a solid supply chain, ramping up its MI350 family last year, securing additional server CPU capacity, and preparing a strong launch of the MI400 series accelerators. Moreover, Team Red has the largest contractual agreements for the AI systems on the market, which provide long-term revenue visibility and a solid backing for the future Instinct roadmap. But given the highly competitive nature of the Helios full rack-scale system, the company could have even more gigawatt deals in the pipeline.

data.title: Runway Growth: The Pieces Are There, But It's Not Time Yet
data.description:
Investment Thesis and Recommendation

While Runway Growth Finance Corp (RWAY) trades at a significant discount and a high yield, I do not believe this is the time for fundamental investors to buy this business development corporation. Speculative investors may find it compelling.

As with all BDCs, RWAY is predominantly an income investment. The primary analytical focus is on the sustainability of net investment income, the quality of the underlying portfolio, and the coverage of the distribution.

  • Credit quality remains strong with 25 basis points of non-accruals as of 12/31/25, which is 25 basis points lower than the year-ago period.
  • NII did not fully cover the dividend as prepayments dropped, and the drop in the associated fees dragged NII lower. NII would have been even lower had the manager not waived a step-up in the base management fee.
  • The externally managed structure and cost is a drag relative to internally managed venture/growth peers. That said, BC Partners and the pending acquisition of SWK Holdings serve to broaden the "deal funnel" for RWAY.

Runway is in the process of acquiring SWK Holdings, which should have multiple benefits for the firm and the portfolio. From the firm side, it brings in expertise in healthcare and life sciences, while on the portfolio level, it adds new sectors and reduces overall position sizes (as a percentage of the portfolio).

The stock trades at roughly 52% of NAV - a very steep discount that reflects both sector headwinds and specific items such as dividend stability, NII generation, and software exposure. At current levels, the yield is approximately 18–19%, which offers an income cushion (assuming the dividend remains intact, which I will discuss further). The discount to NAV creates potential for total return upside if the SWK integration proceeds as expected and the earnings trajectory inflects.

However, until NII coverage is restored above 100% on a sustainable basis and SWK is settled and integrated, I do not see a reason to buy this BDC. As discussed in the "dividend stability" section, unless spillover income is utilized, it is likely the dividend will have to be resized. While it holds the title "most discounted" in the BDC venture growth space and trades with an 18.5% yield, I would look at the internally managed venture/growth BDCs if you want this exposure (I am more of a fundamental investor than a speculator). Most investors might be able to harvest a loss in this name (if they own it) and trade into a more risk balanced growth BDC (admittedly, at a premium to NAV - for now) or another BDC. (This is NOT accounting/tax/investment advice.)

Importantly, however, Runway has been focused on being a "pure" growth/venture BDC. With the acquisition by BC Partners, the focus has begun to expand into areas such as asset-based lending, equipment leasing, and fund finance. It has yet to be seen if the post-BC Partners entity will continue to focus or will broaden their mandate (which would more resemble Trinity Capital). Sector diversification reduces concentration risk but also reduces the differentiation that served as the reason for exposure by some investors. Runway may indeed have what it takes to trade at a lower discount as they build upon BCP and SWK. This BDC bears watching as it appears to be making the right moves.

Bottom Line: A hold/swap for fundamental investors. The discount, the merger impact, and the ability of RWAY to repurchase shares in mid-May (blackout until the merger closes, then a blackout for earnings) is a speculative buy for risk-seeking investors.

If you want exposure to this BDC, you may want to consider their recently issued baby bond (RWAYI). The stripped yield is 7.20% and it will be less volatile. You can find the prospectus here.

Why I feel confident most investors have a loss:

Company Description

Runway Growth Finance is an externally managed BDC focused on venture/growth stage companies. RWAY focuses on the technology, healthcare, business services, financial services, and consumer services segments.

Runway is externally managed by Runway Growth Capital LLC. Runway Growth Capital was acquired by BC Partners Credit (a subsidiary of BC Partners) in 2025. The acquisition by BC has provided the company with a larger "funnel" of potential deals given BC Partners complimentary focus across the private capital stack. It is expected that RWAY will close a previously announced ($220mm) acquisition of SWK Holdings in April 2026. SWK Holdings is focused on lending to a number of verticals within the healthcare sector. The acquisition expands RWAY's exposure to healthcare and life sciences, a beneficial expansion within venture/growth. SWK will bring approximately 13 loans with a fair value of $235 million to the RWAY portfolio.

Parent Company - BC Partners

BC Partners is an alternative investment manager with approximately $40 billion in AUM focused on private equity, private credit, and real estate. BC Partners Credit is the umbrella under which Runway falls. The credit sleeve is focused on middle-market companies across software/tech, business services, healthcare, and other sectors.

BC Partners Credit focuses on lending primarily in North America and Europe. The credit side of the firm has approximately $9B in AUM. The firm has three primary strategies they describe the following way:

  1. Opportunistic - focused on capital solutions, asset-backed financing, and dislocated liquid credit (high yield/distressed).
  2. Yield - senior secured direct lending, both asset-based (lending against pools of assets) and cash flow (traditional credit)-based.
  3. Insurance - strategic solutions within the insurance chain (financial services).

Portfolio Profile

As of December 31, 2025, RWAY's portfolio consisted of approximately $927 million in investments at fair value, invested in 56 portfolio companies (31 within the debt portfolio, the remainder in equity and warrants). The portfolio is overwhelmingly composed of senior secured debt, with first lien loans representing approximately 92% of fair value (4 percentage points higher than YE 2024). Second lien loans, subordinated debt, preferred stock, common stock/warrant positions, and equity constitute the remaining balance. Approximately 76% of the portfolio consists of PE0sponsored companies, with the other 24% being directly sourced. The lower allocation to sponsored companies helps create a differentiated portfolio.

RWAY's portfolio is predominantly first lien (as we would expect).

In June 2025, the company adopted a GICS industry classification methodology, so YE 2025 and 2024 are not directly comparable. What is useful, however, is noting that application software increased marginally during the year and the allocation to the JV with Cadma (described below) increased.

The upcoming acquisition of SWK will help further diversify the portfolio and reduce the weighting of RWAY's existing line items.

As with many BDCs, the company has entered into a JV with another direct lender to create a levered vehicle where they have an equity interest and receive payment from this vehicle (capital efficient as it can be more levered than the BDC itself). Runway's JV partner is Cadma, an affiliate of Apollo (APO). Runway and Cadma have equal ownership of the JV and the target is $70 million in equity capital.

Portfolio Quality

Runway's credit quality is strong, as reported. As of December 31, 2025, only one loan (Mingle Healthcare Solutions, on non-accrual for over a year) remained on non-accrual, with a cost basis of $4.8 million and a fair value of $2.4 million, representing 0.25% of the portfolio at fair value. Since inception, Runway has maintained an average gross credit loss ratio of just 10 basis points per year on cumulative commitments and 7 basis points net of equity recoveries

The weighted average portfolio risk rating increased somewhat to 2.40 from 2.30 from YE 2024, reflecting softness in the software sector. The FV/Cost ratio declined to approximately 97.9% from 99.2% at year-end 2024, partly reflecting the 90.73% system software mark noted below and in the RWAY peer comparison.

One thing to be cognizant of is the amount of PIK income (non-cash, so it can't be used for the dividend payment). It has increased marginally QoQ, but over 300 basis points YoY.

Company comments on software from the Q4 earnings call:

As it pertains to software specifically, we are confident in our current portfolio and continue to be positive on the sector as an investment opportunity. We believe the best companies will not only be able to coexist with AI, but will be able to leverage AI as a tool to optimize their operating models and accelerate penetration in the markets they serve.

More specifically, if you look at our existing portfolio, our software companies share the following characteristics: mission-critical functions with a long diligence and implementation cycle, strong moats defined by domain expertise and high switching costs and customer diversification, which we believe meaningfully derisks revenue growth projections.

The marks on software over the last year:

Software is one of the primary considerations recently, so being aware of their marks is important. If software is being impaired by AI (keep in mind, software is a VERY broad category), one might expect the marks on application software to be similar to those on system software. That said, looking at Hercules Capital (HTGC), which I wrote about here, and Trinity Capital (TRIN), the application software marks are similar, and the system software marks are lower (which will be shown in the "comparison" section).

Liquidity

As of December 31, 2025, RWAY had approximately $395.2 million in total available liquidity, comprising $18.2 million in unrestricted cash and $377.0 million in available borrowing capacity under its credit facility.

Unfunded commitments totaled $145.5 million: $122.8 million in debt and $22.7 million in equity commitments to the CADMA JV. Approximately $32.4 million of unfunded debt commitments are conditioned on milestone achievement (the portfolio company must accomplish some milestone in order to receive more funds) and are (typically) unlikely to be fully drawn. Available liquidity as a multiple of unfunded commitments ($395.2M / $145.5M) is 2.72x, which provides ample headroom to meet portfolio obligations.

Post-quarter, Runway took significant liability management steps: the April 2026 notes (8.54%) were repaid in full on January 21, 2026; $103.25M in 7.25% notes due 2031 were issued in February 2026 (described earlier); and $40.25M of 7.50% 2027 notes plus all $51.75M of 8.00% 2027 notes were redeemed on March 6, 2026 (to my chagrin). These actions reduce near-term maturity concentration, lower the cost of funds, and extend the debt maturity ladder.

Dividend Stability

The base quarterly distribution is $0.33 per share ($1.32 annualized). Q4 2025 NII of $0.32/share produced coverage of 97%—modestly below the payout for the quarter. Spillover income stood at approximately $0.65 per share at year-end 2025, providing roughly two quarters of buffer at the current distribution level, and 9 quarters of protection at a 100-bps stress rate.

Company comments on the dividend (from the earnings call):

With respect to the dividend, we believe it is set at a sustainable level. Our Board will continue to evaluate and approve future distributions, knowing how important consistency is to our fellow shareholders.

The problem is this: The company expects a $0.02 headwind due to the 8% note redemption, prepayments normalized in Q4, leading to lower NII and portfolio yield (prepayment fee income dropped), and spreads remain on the tighter end of the spectrum (yes, there is still lending going on despite the "private credit and AI apocalypse" we have become accustomed to hearing). These factors could continue to limit NII and, therefore, dividend coverage. Should things not change, I can't see why the board will not "right size" the dividend. While share repurchases will be accretive to NII, buying back shares lowers the portfolio value (the capital has to come from lending, as they have implied a 1.3 D/E target and will be bumping up against that target as post-SWK they expect to be 1.2x) and could increase the base management fee.

A "newer" development that could help support the dividend is the outlook for Fed funds. The outlook for 2026 has shifted from 3 (25bp) cuts to no cuts and an increasing probability of a hike. This, of course, is fluid and will be heavily influenced by geopolitical events in the middle East.

Fee Structure

Runway has an interesting fee structure. If assets fall below $1B, the base fee increases from 1.50% to 1.60%. If assets fall below $500MM, the base fee increases to 1.75%. In other words, if they delever or go into a runoff mode, the fee increases. While this creates a tiering structure that is often seen in separate accounts for institutions, this is the first I have seen for a BDC. The 8 percent hurdle and the 80% catch up provision are decent, but the 1.50% base fee is among the higher base fees (and it would be 1.60%, but management waived the increase in Q3). Should the BDC stay below $1 billion and the waiver fall away, this will reduce NII, creating a further NII/dividend headwind.

Comparisons

For comps I have used the other venture/growth BDCs: Hercules Capital (HTGC), Trinity Capital (TRIN), and TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG).

RWAY is significantly smaller on a market cap basis than the two bigger BDCs, TRIN and HTGC, and therefore does not command as much attention. Its leverage will be increasing to be more in line with its peers after SWK closes. PIK, while scary at 14%, isn't too much higher than HTGC and is below TVPG, which has a staggering 20% in PIK (as % of total investment income).

Runway has lower marks on its software portfolio(s) than peers, implying better marks or lower-quality investments. I tend to lean to better marks (although they may all be higher than they should be).

Looking at YTD performance, Runway hasn't performed that badly versus peers (it is important to note this is relative to peers, -21% is pretty horrible) but has underperformed the VanEck BDC ETF (BIZD) by over 10.5%.

Over the last 3 years, Runway has underperformed its larger peers and BIZD significantly.

Runway and TriplePoint are vying for the "most discounted" crown.

Conclusion

Runway's metrics do not support the discount that the BDC trades at, but that alone is not a reason to invest. Runway must shore up their NII or resize their dividend to create more stable income security. While I believe they are headed in the right direction, achieving this goal is not guaranteed.

data.title: 3 Reasons To Buy Newmont
data.description:
Newmont Corporation (NEM) (NGT:CA) is up by over 30% since the last time I checked on the stock in September 2025. A price rise of 35% was penciled in then, so the increase looks exactly in line.

But this big picture view hides the fact that NEM had actually seen a massive decline of almost 30% since the highs of late January. Looked at another way, by January 2026, it had far overshot my projection, rising by over 70% instead. Here, I address why NEM has declined in recent months and why there's upside due for it now.

Why is NEM falling?

We don't need to look any farther than the gold price to figure out why the stock has corrected. The SDPR Gold Shares ETF (GLD), which tracks the prices of gold bullion, is up by just 1% YTD, with NEM staying flat in this time too.

This sounds counterintuitive considering the US-Iran war. It risks higher inflation, and gold has historically been a hedge against runaway price increases. Instead, gold is down by 17% since. This is explained by the weak short-term link between gold and inflation. Relatedly, the likelihood of higher interest rates due to higher inflation also increases the opportunity cost of holding gold.

This trend is due for a reversal, however. And the prospects for NEM look bright over the remainder of 2026. Here are three reasons why.

#1. Gold forecasts indicate healthy sales growth

To start off, gold price forecasts are still rather bullish. Goldman Sachs expects gold to end the year at $5,400/oz, which is a 20% increase from the present levels. My net sales estimates for Newmont based on this projection bode well, indicating a 16% increase. This is a healthy enough increase, even though it's smaller compared to a 21% growth in 2025.

To get to these estimates, I assumed that the realized gold price for the company will continue to be 18.9% below that forecast for the year-end, exactly as was seen in 2025. The reasoning behind a smaller realized price is, of course, that it's an average over the year. This results in an assumption for a realized gold price of $4,382/oz. With the company's gold production guidance of 5.3 million ounces, the gross gold sales revenue comes to $23.2 billion.

Newmont reports net sales, which were 3.2% smaller than the implied gross sales from realized prices and production in 2025. The same is assumed for 2026, resulting in net gold sales of $22.5 billion. Next, much as seen last year, net gold sales as a percentage of the total are also expected to remain unchanged at 85.2% in 2026, resulting in a total sales number of $26.4 billion.

#2. Market multiples stay attractive

Assuming that the company's adjusted net margin remains unchanged at 33.5%, the same as in 2025, the adjusted net income figure comes to $8.85 billion. This in turn gives a forward non-GAAP P/E of 12.5x. This compares positively with the stock's five-year average of 18x, indicating over 40% upside in 2026 alone.

It's worth adding here that my estimates are conservative compared with the average of analysts' estimates on Seeking Alpha. Based on diluted weighted average shares seen in Q4 2025, the adjusted EPS comes to $8.1, as per my estimates. By comparison, analysts estimates are 17% higher at $9.7, resulting in a forward non-GAAP P/E of 10.8x. This in turn indicates an upside of over 60%.

#3. Share buybacks and dividend increase

There are also the passive gains to consider from an investment in NEM. Between 2026 and 2027, the company plans to repurchase shares worth $2.4 billion. This is 2.2% of the current market capitalization and, spread over a period of two years, doesn't sound like much. But even then, it adds to the expected price returns.

Further, along with its full-year 2025 results, the company raised its dividends per quarter by 4% to $0.26. Assuming that it remains the same in each of the succeeding three quarters of the year, the forward dividend yield comes to 1%. Much like the buybacks, this isn't much at all either. NEM's five-year average forward dividend yield is at a much higher 3%, and the average yield for the materials sector is also higher at 1.8%.

But over time, the returns add up. As the chart below shows, over a five-year period, total returns exceed price returns by 27 percentage points.

What can go wrong

The projected returns for 2026 do come attached with risks, though. The big one being the gold price forecast. If gold price doesn't rise as predicted, the company's financials for this year might not end up as robust.

For example, consider what happens if the realized gold price remains unchanged from last year. Owing to smaller expected production, the revenue will drop by around 1% this year. Assuming an unchanged adjusted net margin from 2025, the adjusted net income would decline by 7%. The forward P/E then comes to 15.6x, which still implies upside. But to a far lesser extent of around 15%.

I do believe, however, that the gold price is likely due some uptick from here on. Take a look at the long-term gold price chart below. It shows that between 2013 and 2019, the yellow metal's price went nowhere, coinciding with a period of muted inflation trends. While the Fed's forecasts still point to manageable inflation this year, the projection is at risk considering that crude prices are showing no signs of letting up.

What next?

Even if oil prices and the risk to inflation do subside, and gold prices fall further, even the pessimistic forecast shows upside to NEM this year from a fundamental perspective. And if they don't, it goes without saying that buying the safest asset around is in order. Whichever way I look at it, the stock continues to look attractive. I'm retaining a Buy rating.

data.title: The Return Of Friction
data.description:
The market is asking the wrong question. The debate over whether a war-driven oil spike counts as “true inflation” or merely a temporary supply shock is too narrow to be useful. It is academically neat and strategically incomplete. What matters is not the label. What matters is how far the shock travels once it hits a system that had grown accustomed to cheap energy, cheap money, and easy assumptions about liquidity.

That distinction matters because markets do not price textbook categories. They price transmission. A supply shock may not qualify as inflation in the strict monetary sense, but it can still reprice inflation expectations, alter central bank reaction functions, compress real incomes, tighten financial conditions, and weaken growth before demand has any chance to adjust. The argument, then, should not be framed as inflation versus supply shock. The more relevant question is whether the shock changes the operating conditions of the economy. And in this case, it does.

Energy is not just an input. It is the economy’s conversion layer

The reason is simple. Energy is not just another input in the system. It is the systems conversion layer. It is what allows labor, logistics, fertilizer, transport, cooling, manufacturing, and computation to become output. A persistent energy shock, therefore, does more than lift a few line items in the inflation basket. It raises the real cost of moving the entire economy. In that sense, it functions less like a headline CPI event and more like a tax on productivity.

That is the deeper point. When energy becomes scarcer or more expensive for long enough, the same workforce, the same factories, the same trucks, the same farms, and the same data centers produce less real surplus. More of society's resources must be devoted simply to keeping the machine running. Households are left with less discretionary purchasing power. Firms discover that what looked like healthy margns in a benign cost environment were often just a byproduct of cheap throughput. Agriculture becomes newly vulnerable through fertilizer, diesel, transport, and refrigeration. Europe, already more exposed to imported energy costs and policy fragmentation, begins to weaken faster than the US. The issue is not only that prices go up. It is that the economy’s conversion efficiency falls.

Once framed this way, a great many seemingly disconnected developments start to rhyme. Weakening PMIs are no longer just data noise. They are the first statistical trace of a system losing momentum while its cost base is still rising. Luxury resale markets matter not because handbags and collector cars suddenly become macro assets, but because they are sensitive barometers of real surplus. Gated property funds matter not because every liquidity gate is a new Lehman, but because they reveal the gap between smooth marks and true clearing prices. Private credit becomes more fragile for the same reason: it is a part of the market that depends on stable funding, patient capital, and the ability to defer recognition. A world of higher friction makes all three less reliable.

Productivity shocks do not stay in the real economy. They migrate into finance

And that is where the real migration occurs. Productivity shocks do not stay neatly contained in the real economy. They travel into finance. A sustained energy shock eventually becomes a funding shock, a credit shock, and a price discovery shock.

The funding channel is the first place to look. More expensive energy raises the working capital needs of the global system. In a dollar-based world, it can also increase the demand for dollar balance sheet even without a dramatic headline move in the dollar index. That is why physical bottlenecks can become monetary bottlenecks. The visible shock in commodity prices may understate the stress building in the plumbing.

The credit channel follows naturally. Higher input costs, weaker demand, and tighter financial conditions impair margins and refinancing assumptions at the same time. Borrowers that looked serviceable in a world of cheap energy and abundant liquidity begin to look fragile in a world where both are scarcer. This does not always show up first in public credit spreads. Often it shows up in amemdment activity, delayed restructurings, rising nonaccruals, and a general resistance to mark assets where they would actually clear. Illiquidity delays pain, but it does not erase it.

That same logic explains why delayed mark-to-market sectors deserve so much attention here. Commercial real estate, private credit, semi-liquid funds, certain corners of venture, and even parts of luxury resale all share a common vulnerability. They depend, to varying degrees, on a social fiction that smooth marks can substitute for real liquidity. That fiction works best in a low-friction regime. It works much less well when physical stress, funding stress, and redemption pressure begin to collide. What looks stable in private markets is often just deferred volatility.

The winners are not generic inflation hedges. They are control points

This is why the most useful investment response is not to reach for the standard menu of inflation hedges or generic defensives. That response is too blunt for the regime that may be emerging. The more important question is where value migrates in a world of rising friction. And the answer, in broad terms, is that value tends to move toward control points.

Assets with real scarcity matter more. So do businesses with pricing power, short-duration cash flows, strong balance sheets, low dependence on external funding, and some control over the bottlenecks that are now being repriced. In a low-friction regime, the market can afford to reward long-duration promises, smooth marks, and stories that assume abundant throughput. In a high-friction regime, those assumptions become less credible. The winners are less likely to be the broad category of “inflation hedges” and more likely to be the assets that can preserve economics when energy, funding, logistics, and price discovery all become harsher.

That is an important distinction. The likely losers are not simply growth stocks or risk assets in the abstract. They are the assets whose valuations implicitly depended on cheap energy, cheap capital, patient financing, and the ability to postpone reality. The likely winners are not simply commodity proxies. They are the assets that sit at real chokepoints and can convert scarcity into durable economics without needing fragile markets to validate them every quarter.

Seen that way, the macro story becomes broader and more coherent. It is not really about whether the 1970s are back. It is about the return of friction. Friction in energy. Friction in logistics. Friction in funding. Friction in credit. Friction in valuation. Friction in price discovery. The common thread is not that the world is about to collapse overnight. It is that a system built on smoothness is being forced to rediscover roughness.

That is the real regime shift. A world organized around cheap energy, cheap money, and smooth marks is being challenged by a world of bottlenecks, fragmentation, and forced price discovery. That does not guarantee immediate systemic crisis. But it does mean that the hurdle rate for almost every asset is quietly moving higher.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Upstart: Bank Charter Is The Future
data.description:
Upstart Holdings, Inc. (UPST) was poised to be a big AI winner, but the company has failed to fully re-launch the AI lending marketplace the last couple of years. The company has faced artificial pain with a reliance on investing partners in order to fund loans no matter how great the loan performance. My investment thesis is far more Bullish on the stock dip to the prior lows around $20 while the company pursues a promising bank charter.

Bank Charter Opportunity

Since online lending platforms started in at least the 2010s, the biggest issue has been the loss of funding sources during economic weakness. Upstart felt this issue back in 2022 and the business has never fully recovered despite expansion into other lending segments beyond personal loans.

Both LendingClub (LC) and SoFi (SOFI) obtained banking charters to solve a big part of the issues with reliance on outside funding sources. LendingClub started with the same original plan of Upstart to rely on fees of originating and/or servicing personal loans issued on the platform, but the fintech eventually realized no matter how successful the loan performance, lending partners had other goals and risk tolerance.

The goal of generating fee income without taking credit risk was attractive, but the business is hostage to other financial partners. If the loans are actually performing strong, the fintech should just hold the loans and participate in the profits from the interest income.

For this reason, the biggest negative on Upstart was the lack of investing in loans when external loan funds dried up. On March 10, the fintech announced plans to pursue a national bank charter, though the company appeared to back away from truly expanding into lending to consumers directly using customer deposits.

In the press release, President and Chief Capital Officer Sanjay Datta had the following to say about the bank charter:

Banks, credit unions, and institutional funds will continue to be the capital source for the vast majority of all loans originated on the Upstart platform. We are not seeking to compete with our depository partners for local customer deposits and checking accounts.

Upstart appears to suggest part of the goal is a streamlined regulatory framework. The company suggests the Upstart Bank can access deposit funding, but the company isn't going to compete with banking partners and doesn't place much emphasis on investing in loans originated on the platform.

As of the end of 2025, Upstart had nearly $1 billion invested in loans held by the bank. The company invests primarily in R&D loans in new products like the ramping business in auto and home originations, but management has always mentioned a goal of reducing the loan balances.

For Q4'25, the fintech had $61 million in interest income with $31 million in net income after interest expense and fair value adjustments. The guidance for 2026 was only net interest income of ~$100 million, so the current business is minimal.

Fallen Very Far

The stock has basically traded at these lows since back in 2022. Upstart saw quarterly revenue reach $300 million back at the end of 2021 and ended 2025 with revenues back to the prior high.

The fintech now forecasts 2026 revenues of only $1.4 billion and forecasts a 35% revenue growth rate from 2025 through 2028. The crazy part is that Upstart traded up to nearly $400 back in late 2021 and the fintech is ready to roll past the prior peak levels while the stock is at only $25 now.

Upstart doesn't even trade at 2x sales now and the stock is even cheaper with an EBITDA goal of nearly $300 million. Upstart trades at less than 10x adjusted EBITDA estimates with a market cap at $2.5 billion.

The outlook has a terminal EBITDA margin target of 25% with a revenue goal amounting to nearly $2.5 billion. The stock only trades at 1x this revenue target while the terminal EBITDA goal is $625 million, or just 4x the market cap.

Note, the financial targets were provided before Upstart announced plans to launch a national bank. If the company was to become more aggressive with funding loans via deposits, the AI lending marketplace could grow revenues at a faster clip and reduce risk of losing funding partners. Also, a higher focus on interest income reduces revenue volatility.

Since the banking charter will take time to obtain regulatory approval, Upstart is highly reliant on banking partners and private credit investors. The company just announced a forward flow agreement for $1 billion, but Upstart could ultimately face some pressure as private credit investors face redemptions.

Ultimately, the biggest risk is a weak economic period and rising credit losses. If the AI lending marketplace doesn't produce superior returns for a period, Upstart will lose institutional and bank partner funds.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Upstart is far too cheap here. The AI lending marketplace has a potential strong catalyst with a future bank charter while the stock is back to the lows. Either way Upstart appears set to report record revenues and profits in 2026 and the stock shouldn't trade at the lows.

data.title: Amazon: Short-Term Pressure, Long-Term Opportunity
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) has seen a small dip in its share price, recently prompting me to write this article. Amazon’s fundamentals are very strong: In FY25, Amazon's revenue grew by 12% to $716.9 billion, operating income to roughly $80.0B, and AWS revenue to $128.7B. In Q4 2025, AWS grew 24%, its fastest growth in 13 quarters, while advertising grew 22%, according to management. Andy Jassy, CEO of Amazon, also strongly believes that AWS can reach $600 billion in annual revenue by 2036. AWS is also the world's largest cloud provider, taking up about 28-30% of market share. For these reasons and others that I will cover, I rate AMZN as a Strong Buy.

This doesn’t go without risk. Amazon is in one of the most competitive industries in the world right now. Although they are the number 1 cloud provider, Microsoft (MSFT) Azure and Alphabet's (GOOG) Google Cloud are creeping up quickly. There is also a risk that Amazon is overspending on AI, and their free cash flows may remain depressed and crunch their return on invested capital. More of this below.

Financial Overview

In FY25, Amazon performed very well. Total revenue was $716.9B, up from $638.0B in FY24. Operating income and net income also saw growth in FY25, now roughly $80.0B and $77.7B roughly. Even more notably, operating and net margins are approximately 11.2% and 10.8% in FY25, up from 10.8% and 9.3% in FY24.

Let’s get into the main reasons why Amazon has seen a strong FY25 performance. Amazon’s biggest driver remains to be AWS, up 20% YoY and 24% in Q4 2025. Driven by cloud demand, AI workloads, and enterprise adoption. This is Amazon’s growth engine, and I believe this is just the beginning for AWS’ expansion because of their AI investments.

In FY25, Amazon’s advertising is another high-growth, high-margin segment, being 22%, says management. This explains that there are more sellers on Amazon and higher monetization per user. Amazon’s retail business is more modest in comparison to AWS and advertising but has still seen double-digit growth. North American and International retail have both grown by 10% and 13% in FY25, driven by faster deliveries, more prime usage, and lower pricing compared to competitors.

In the chart above, we can see the main concern is their free cash flow (FCF). Although Amazon’s cash flow from operations has grown to $139.5B in Q4 2025, Amazon’s capex has risen dramatically higher. Their PP&E spending was $131.9B, offset by $3.5B in proceeds and incentives, making net PP&E $128.3B. Operating profitability improved greatly, but FCF clearly did not. However, this is not a stress point for me as an investor. Amazon is taking calculated, deliberate, and prudent bets on real AI demand in the market. In 2026, capex is expected to be roughly $200B. A depressed FCF is a near-term issue equaling high risk, high reward.

Metrics & Valuation

Let’s take a look at Amazon’s current return on invested capital (ROIC) because they have already been spending heavily on AI and are very capital intensive. Amazon’s EBIT is currently $80B, making their net operating profit after tax roughly $64B. We’ll divide this number by their total assets, cash, and operating liabilities, which are $818B, $86.8B, and $218B. Subtracting these numbers gives us $513 for the denominator. This makes Amazon’s ROIC roughly $64 / $513 = 12.5%. This is a solid percentage and is above their WACC, roughly 9% as an estimate, displaying solid growth creation. It is also important to note that this percentage is pulled down due to heavy spending, making Amazon’s future ROIC potentially higher if their investments pan out, which I have conviction they will because demand isn’t going anywhere.

In FY25, Amazon’s earnings per share were $7.17. Up from $5.53 the year prior and $2.90 in FY23.

As of this article, Amazon currently trades at roughly $200, making Amazon’s price-to-earnings ratio roughly 27x.

On a forward earnings basis, Amazon appears relatively expensive, trading at 25-26x earnings for 8% expected EPS growth, compared to peers like Meta Platforms (META), which offers significantly higher growth at a lower multiple, 17-18x and 28.74% expected growth. But there is nuance to this. Amazon’s earnings are temporarily depressed right now due to capex surges and their FCF dropping. This directly leads to EPS, margins, and ROIC dipping in the short term. Microsoft (MSFT) will also see a dip in P/E but with slower earnings growth of 5%, and Google (GOOGL) is expected to have a 24x multiple with 7% growth.

The difference between Amazon and a company like Meta who has a low forward P/E and high expected EPS growth, is that investments are happening alongside strong earnings growth for Meta, while investments are happening at the expense of current earnings for Amazon. Amazon’s relatively lower forward EPS growth compared to peers such as Meta is not a reflection of weaker demand but rather a result of its more aggressive reinvestment strategy.

This does not cause a value investor like myself to fret. Rather, I am even more optimistic. Amazon looks like it’s trading at more of a premium due to a high forward P/E, making Amazon less desirable in some investors eyes. But as some investors become cautious about this stock, I’ll remain bullish. FY25 and Q4 2025 financials were solid, but in the short term, earnings and FCF may be depressed, and that’s more than OK because Amazon is a long-term investment capitalizing on real AI demand in the market while still having a solid cloud and retail business.

Forward Looking & Consensus

Briefly looking at Amazon’s consensus EPS estimates, we can see that over the next year, EPS growth will be slower compared to expected future years due to heavy investments.

As FCF and capex spending normalize, EPS will increase as a result.

Taking a look at some analysts' expectations, most major investment firms are bullish on Amazon and expect an outperformance. Citigroup, UBS, Barclays, and BMO Capital expect Amazon’s stock price in a year’s time to be roughly from $285 to the highest estimate of $310. Currently, the average analyst price target is $281.34.

I agree with analysts' estimates for reasons I have explained throughout this article but will reiterate: strong financials, heavy investments in real AI demand, solid and growing AWS, retail, and advertising segments, and cloud market dominance.

Risk To Thesis

The primary risk to this thesis is that Amazon’s elevated capex may not generate the level of returns currently implied by management and investor expectations. The company is investing aggressively in AI infrastructure, with capex expected to reach approximately $200 billion in 2026, following already significant spending in 2025.

If demand for AI and cloud services does not scale as anticipated, or if pricing pressure grows due to competition from Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud, Amazon may face a long period of depressed FCF and weaker ROIC. In this scenario, the current reinvestment cycle would not translate into the expected future earnings expansion, and Amazon’s valuation could remain compressed or decline.

Additionally, while AWS remains the largest cloud provider, their growth advantage has narrowed relative to peers, increasing the risk that Amazon must continue spending heavily simply to maintain its market position. This could result in structurally lower margins than investors expect.

If Amazon fails to achieve sufficient scale, their earnings and FCF may not normalize as expected, making Amazon’s premium valuation hard to justify.

Conclusion

Amazon may look expensive on the surface, but that’s because it’s aggressively investing in AI and cloud infrastructure rather than maximizing short-term profits. In my view, that’s exactly what you want to see.

Current earnings and FCF don’t fully reflect the company’s true earning power. As these investments begin to scale, I expect meaningful reacceleration in growth.

With strong fundamentals, clear leadership in AWS, and multiple high-margin growth drivers, I see the current setup as a compelling long-term opportunity and a Strong Buy.

data.title: Celsius: Chug Or Sip?
data.description:
As an avid energy drink enjoyer, I tend to try out many new flavors and brands when they come out. One brand that I have tried many flavors of is Celsius (CELH), which is the subject of this article. Celsius is a challenger in the energy drink market, setting itself apart by using more natural ingredients. The question that will be explored in this article is whether this is enough to make it a good investment.

Company Overview

Celsius is a Nevada-incorporated, Florida-headquartered energy drink company. The company's main brand, Celsius, was founded in 2005 but did not really get into prominence until the Covid lockdowns. Where other challengers managed to get a foot in the door in the energy drink market but subsequently failed (such as Bang), Celsius managed to take a decent market share from Monster (MNST) and Red Bull. Last year, the company also completed the acquisitions of Alani Nu and Rockstar (from Pepsi (PEP)), making it the largest competitor to the incumbents.

Even though the acquired brands are also active in the energy drink sector, they are to an extent complementary. The Celsius brand mostly focuses on fitness/wellness consumers, whereas Alani Nu mainly focuses on female consumers and Rockstar on the more "traditional" energy drink consumers.

While the ready-to-drink (RTD) category is by far the largest, the company also sells on-the-go powder & hydration sticks. The advantage of the sticks is that the costs to manufacture are cheaper as they do not require the aluminum cans, nor do they need to add the sparkling water. Personally, I prefer the sticks, as they tend to be highly concentrated, and 1 stick can make up to 2 liters of energy drink. Besides the energy drinks and derivatives, the company also sells nutrition and wellness products. This is the result of the acquisition of Alani Nu, which was already selling things like protein drinks and pre-workout.

Most of these products aren't produced in-house, though, as the company primarily uses co-packers for manufacturing (it does have its own facility due to the acquisition of Big Beverages in 2024). In the US & Canada, the manufacturing and distribution are handled by Pepsi (for Alani Nu since Q4 2025 and for Celsius since 2024). Internationally, it tends to be handled by the Suntory Group, a Japanese company that also holds the license for Pepsi Japan.

Pepsi Partnership

While briefly touched upon above, Celsius and Pepsi have a strong partnership also called "the Captaincy." The captaincy was the result of a transaction between the two companies in which Celsius acquired the rights to be the strategist of the energy drink sales in North America, bought Rockstar, and issued Pepsi convertible preferred stock. Under the Captaincy, Pepsi is required to use "commercially reasonable efforts to sell and distribute the products of the Celsius brands." The proceeds were approximately $935.8 million, of which approximately $307 million for Rockstar. As a result of the newly issued convertible preferred stock, Pepsi is also allowed to add an additional board member to the board of Celsius.

Personally, I am a bit on the fence about this transaction. The reason for this is that, effectively, Celsius paid Pepsi $599 million to sell Celsius' own product. While it obviously is a bit more nuanced, it seems like Pepsi got the better end of the deal.

Governance

In terms of governance, the company had 10 directors at the end of 2025, two of whom are linked to Pepsi (Christy Jacoby and John Short). Out of the other 8, 7 are independent. A board with more independent directors tends to be favorable, as they are more objective (they are not judging their own work), are more likely to protect minority shareholders, and tend to focus more on the longer term.

Another interesting thing to look at is insider activity. Over the past 12 months, there were significantly more sales than buys. Additionally, many of the shares bought were the result of RSUs, options, etc. At the same time, the CEO has a 10b5-1 trading plan for 792k shares. This allows him to sell on a predetermined schedule, as he has access to material non-public information.

For a company that, to an extent, signals that it is undervalued, the executives do not seem to put their money where their mouth is, which is a bit disappointing.

Industry and Competitive Landscape

As mentioned before, Celsius is the third-largest company in the energy drink sector. The largest company in 2025 was Red Bull, with a market share of 35.9%, trailed by Monster with a market share of 27.3% (ex. other brands like Reign). Behind Celsius (including all brands at 19.8%, as mentioned before), there are a bunch of smaller companies which account for the remaining 17%.

The energy drink market is also growing, and according to research from Ipsos, approximately 19% of Americans consume energy drinks with caffeine, and 3% drink energy drinks without caffeine. One of the reasons why energy drinks and other caffeinated drinks have won in popularity is the relatively high price of coffee.

The reason why consumers drink caffeine is to get them through the morning or the afternoon. Many of the younger generation (18-34) wish caffeinated drinks came in smaller sizes. Something that is especially favorable for Red Bull, which mainly sells 8.4fl oz cans, and to a lesser extent Celsius & Alani Nu (12fl oz) versus Monster & Rockstar (16 fl oz).

Another trend that is favorable for Celsius is that approximately 58% of US consumers are paying attention to the ingredients in the drinks (Celsius claims to have natural ingredients), 52% are willing to pay more for drinks that support their fitness goals, and 66% tend to choose lower-sugar and lower-calorie drinks.

On a generation-by-generation basis, the highest usage of energy drinks can be found among Gen Z (18-28 years old), followed by Millennials (29-44). Interestingly, alcohol usage is much lower.

7 Powers

I recently read the book the 7 Powers by Hamilton Helmer. I think it does an excellent job in explaining what could set a company apart from its competitors. For Celsius, I have identified two main ones:

Counter positioning: This used to be the main one for Celsius. It positioned itself on the opposite side of Monster and Red Bull by having natural products, low sugar content, and a different target group (fitness and wellness). Red Bull and Monster couldn't easily change this without undermining their core brands. This was especially successful at the beginning when Celsius took a large chunk of market share. Red Bull and Monster have since adapted, and by acquiring Rockstar, Celsius is no longer only focused on the health-conscious consumer. While the brand is still doing well in terms of counter positioning, its power is weakening. At the same time, the company is getting scale, so it might be that the company is losing its counter-positioning power and instead will have a scale economies power.

Branding: Branding in the 7 Powers is not merely awareness; it is the ability to charge a premium based on how the product makes consumers feel. For Celsius, this is especially how consumers feel with regard to, for example, fitness. People that drink a Celsius energy drink might feel like they are making a better choice than when they would grab a Monster. This is not easily replicable, as the company has built this image over time. For Alani Nu, the branding has the same prevalence but towards a female audience. The only brand of the company that does not possess this power is Rockstar, which has been degrading over time. The strength of the brand also makes me less worried about the Kirkland knockoff from Costco (COST). In Europe, Lidl, Aldi, and many other grocery stores have knockoffs of Monster and Red Bull which are similar to a degree but not the same. Even though these knockoffs are significantly cheaper, Monster and Red Bull still hold the largest market shares. As such, the recent decline in share price seems like an overreaction.

Financials

After establishing a qualitative understanding of the business, it is time to dive into the numbers. I prefer to look at a few key components: revenue growth, margins, and debt.

Revenue growth

For Celsius, the growth rate has been quite volatile over the past few years. Initially it was growing very fast, coming from a low base. When it switched to Pepsi for its distribution and Pepsi made a mistake in terms of how much to order, the growth rate suffered. From that perspective, 2025 looks much better, but it was influenced by two acquisitions. Without the acquisitions, the revenue growth rate would be much lower. Going forward I expect the growth rate to be much lower as well due to the size the company has reached.

Margins

In terms of margins, I tend to compare it to the closest competitors. For energy drinks, a lot of emphasis is placed on the gross margins. Unfortunately, Celsius' margin definition is different than Monster's, and as such they are not fully comparable. Effectively, Celsius includes freight costs in their margins, and Monster does not. If we adjusted for this, then Celsius' margin would be 55.3% vs. Monster's 55.8%. Another thing that is a relatively good predictor of margins is the cost of Aluminum. In the graph below, I charted them against each other, and while the relationship isn't as steady as it used to be, there is still a correlation.

Debt

The final thing to look at is the debt of the company. To acquire Alani Nu, the company went from zero debt to approximately $700m in gross debt. The company has a BB- rating, meaning they are below investment grade, something we do not like to see. They have already refinanced the initial term loan at a rate 75 bp below the initial term loan, which is a positive. Unfortunately, the debt is still at a variable rate, and a 1 percentage point increase in rates adds approximately $6.9m in annual interest expenses. On the other hand, the maturity ladder is manageable, as they only pay around $7m a year in principal.

Valuation

While understanding the company is useful, you also need to know if what you are paying is reasonable. To do this, I like to use three methods: a DCF calculation, an APV calculation, and trading multiples.

DCF

For Celsius, I forecasted revenue growth based on their market share in the different markets they are active in. For the US, I also split it between the different brands. For 2026 I expect a slightly higher earnings growth due to the fact that both Rockstar and Alani Nu will be included for the entire year. I also expect a slightly higher growth in a few years due to more focus on internationalization. For now I have put a slightly higher growth rate in 2028, but this could be earlier or later. I also expect an improvement in cost; last year was impacted by integration costs, and this year I expect costs to be impacted by higher freight and aluminum costs. In the end this led to the following forecast:

The WACC was determined based on the risk-free rate, the company’s cost of debt, and a risk premium. I use an average approach based on the capital structure of the peers that were also used in the financial section, the company itself, the beta of the company, and the beta of peers, and this led to a WACC of 7.23%. The relatively lower WACC compared to its historical cost of equity is the result of the slightly larger peers that are included (such as Monster, Coca-Cola (KO), and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP)) and expected stabilization of growth over time.

With an expected growth in perpetuity of 2.9%, slightly below GDP, the DCF produced the following share price estimate:

As DCF models are sensitive to input parameters, I also created a sensitivity table, which looks like this:

APV

The APV model is similar to the DCF but takes into account tax shields and distress probabilities. For Celsius, given their BB- rating, the distress probability is 19.7%. The potential enterprise value is quite high due to the large amount of goodwill on the balance sheet.

For the APV, I also created a sensitivity table to see what a change in input parameter would do to the estimated value.

Multiples

For the multiples, I used the same peers as for the WACC (MNST, KO, KDP, National Beverages (FIZZ), Molson Coors (TAP), and Vita Coco (COCO)). Forward multiples are based on analyst expectations. Taking the median multiple based on the company’s peers leads to a share price of $31.61. One can argue that this undervalues the company, as many of these do not show the same growth as Celsius. Which I agree with. As such, I assigned the multiples a lower weight in the final valuation.

If we take the average of the DCF method, the APV method, and the combined multiples median (at a 0.5 weight), we arrive at a price target of $43.01, or approximately 26.7% above the price at the moment of writing.

Catalysts

A company without a catalyst can be trading at a cheap valuation for a long time. As such, it is important to look for events that can increase the stock price in the short to mid-term.

Alani Nu growth

One of the main growth stories for Celsius has been the acquisition of Alani Nu. I think that the growth story for Alani Nu is far from done as more (female) consumers are switching from other caffeine drinks (such as coffee) to energy drinks. As the brand is very active with female influencers, these new customers might be more drawn to the Alani Nu brand. Personally, I have to admit that I also prefer the flavors that they bring out under the Alani Nu brand over the flavors they bring out under the Celsius brand.

International

Another catalyst, which is more for the mid-term, is the under-penetration of the international markets. Celsius holds very small market shares outside of North America, and these markets tend to grow faster as energy drinks are not as widespread as in the US. I think that once the company starts to increase its focus abroad and start selling Alani Nu, it will be able to gain some market share in faster-growing markets.

Risks

Celsius brand is stalling, and Rockstar does not help

One of the main risks right now is that the Celsius brand seems to stall. Full-year 2025 core Celsius brand revenue growth was only 7.5%. Additionally, this 7.5% was also compared to the year in which Pepsi made some ordering mistakes. As such, the comps were already very low, and they barely beat it. The acquired Rockstar brand does not help much, as the brand has been slowly degrading over time and needs a revamp. It remains to be seen if Celsius is able to turn this around.

Variable-rate debt

Another risk given the current macro environment is the variable rate debt. As mentioned before, a 1 percentage point increase in rates adds an additional $6.9m in interest expenses. As I do not expect the macro environment to get much better any time soon, there is a high chance that the interest expense will increase significantly in the near future.

Conclusion

Celsius is a fast-growing beverage company that has recently improved its position as the third-largest energy drink company by acquiring two competitors. The company has also deepened its collaboration with Pepsi, for which it now controls the distribution and promotion strategy of the energy drink segment. Nevertheless, this came at a cost, as it had to pay for these rights, and it also acquired the degrading Rockstar brand.

The partnership with Pepsi has also led to more volatile revenue growth, despite a growing market and market share, and margins remain heavily impacted by aluminum prices. Furthermore, the acquisitions have led to a higher debt load, for which the company pays a variable rate.

Despite all of this, the company is operating from a position of strength with strong brand power and a strong but decreasing counter-positioning power. Even so, the stock seems to be undervalued, with a conservative estimate of $43.01 per share. Catalysts include further growth in the Alani Nu brand and international expansion, while risks are the declining growth in the core Celsius brand and the acquisition of Rockstar and the exposure to variable rate debt. Taking all of this into account, I think that the positives outweigh the negatives and assign the company a buy.

data.title: Oil Price Surge Hits German Consumers As Headline Inflation Reaches A Two-Year High
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Soaring energy prices have pushed German headline inflation close to 3%

This shouldn’t be a surprise to anyone. As the war in the Middle East has entered its fifth week and both energy prices and uncertainty remain high, German headline inflation reached its highest level since early 2024 in March.

While the national inflation measure came in at 2.7% year-on-year, from a still innocent 1.9% YoY in February, the European measure surged to 2.8% YoY, from 2.0% YoY. At 1.2% month-on-month, March saw the largest monthly price increase since 2022.

At the same time, however, today’s numbers also showed that for the time being, the inflation shock remains limited to energy prices, as core inflation and services inflation remained unchanged at 2.5% YoY and 3.2% YoY, respectively.

First inflation wave clearly on its way

Oil prices have always been the most important and direct link between geopolitical developments and the real economy. While the war in the Middle East and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz provide further evidence of shifting geopolitics and will have longer-term implications for the European economy, the rise in energy prices is already very real. In Germany, if gasoline prices remained at their current levels until the end of the year, the loss in purchasing power for consumers would already be larger than in 2022.

Today’s inflation data shows that a first inflation wave is clearly on its way. While currently available regional data suggests that the inflation surge in March was mainly driven by energy prices, knock-on effects on transportation costs, food prices and other industrial products will follow. The only question is whether this will be a single, time-limited wave or whether it will eventually also lead to a de-anchoring of inflation expectations and higher wages. However, even if it is only ‘one’ large inflation wave, German inflation should increase further, remaining in the 3% to 4% range for most of the year.

What's next for the ECB?

When looking at possible next steps for the European Central Bank, markets have started to price in up to four rate hikes this year. Indeed, the central bank made a hawkish pivot at the last policy meeting – but we think that markets are too much guided by an overly simplistic reading of the 2022 episode and the narrative that the ECB was far too late reacting to an oil price shock. In our view, however, there are important differences between the current situation and 2022.

Back then, the ECB was emerging from an extremely accommodative stance and normalising policy from negative interest rates and quantitative easing. With hindsight, the biggest policy mistake was probably the delayed response to an energy price shock that ultimately morphed into a broader inflation surge. Learning from that episode, though, does not mean a rate hike is imminent.

As long as the energy price shock remains broadly contained – including first‑round knock-on effects – it remains far from certain that the ECB will react at all. For rate hikes to come back to the table, the Bank would need to see a rise in inflation expectations and a broadening of inflationary pressures across the economy. So far, the war in the Middle East has instead weighed on business and consumer confidence. Meanwhile, the labour market is entering this energy shock in a weaker position than in 2022, and governments’ fiscal pockets are more constrained, making large‑scale stimulus to offset higher energy prices less likely.

Learning from the Easter Bunny

Maybe market participants can learn some lessons from the Easter Bunny. Due to surging prices for cocoa last year, chocolate Easter bunnies have seen their prices increase by up to 30% compared with 2025. A textbook pass-through of higher production costs, one could think. However, this analyst noticed that German supermarkets, at least, have already started special sales of chocolate Easter bunnies ahead of the holiday, rather than the traditional post-Easter dump sale – suggesting that overly high prices can eventually break consumer demand. Maybe that's a first indication that household balance sheets are simply so stretched that any energy price pass-through will be much less pronounced this year than in 2022.

Three potential pain points for the ECB

Looking ahead, three potential pain points remain for the ECB: a psychological one, i.e., headline inflation above 4%, reviving uncomfortable memories of 2022; an analytical one, with core inflation above 3%, signalling broader price pressures; and a credibility one, i.e., a surge in survey‑based inflation expectations, which would constrain the ECB's room for inaction.

Even if the latest developments in the war and in energy prices have started to shift our base case scenario more towards our adverse scenario, we find it hard to see the ECB moving at the next meeting at the end of April. By then, there will be no new forecasts, and only limited data available: March inflation, a handful of country inflation releases for April, and initial estimates of first‑quarter GDP on the day of the meeting. In all honesty, that does not look sufficient to move the needle, unless the ghosts of 2022 are really keeping policymakers awake at night.

For the June meeting, the story is different. Simply put, if we are still talking about the war in the Middle East and high energy prices by then, a rate hike is clearly possible. However, this is not our base case, as we expect the Strait of Hormuz to reopen before then.

Transitory for now but broadening of price shock could still trigger rate hike

All in all, today's German inflation data shows that, for the time being, the new inflation wave stemming from the war in the Middle East is 'only' an energy price shock. Any ECB reaction to this new price shock will clearly depend on whether soaring energy prices will find their way into the rest of the economy or not. Needless to say that a broadening of inflationary pressures and a de-anchoring of inflation expectations could trigger an ECB rate hike. For now, however – at the risk of using a forbidden word, at least at the ECB – the energy price shock falls under the label of 'transitory'.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
